texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Every year, 30 billion ton of CO2 is released to the atmosphere, mainly contributed by combusted gases and burning of fuels [1]. Scientists had estimated that the CO2 concentration would be increased to 970 ppm in the atmosphere by next century [2,3]. CO2 present in fl ue gases is the main contributor to climate change and ocean acidi fi cation [4 -6]. Subsequently, CO2 present in natural gas reduces its energy and cause equipment corrosion in the presence of water [7]. Hence, an economically viable technology for CO2 separating from fl ue gas and natural gas is necessary [8]. Typically, traditional separation technologies demonstrated the disadvantages such as high capital cost, equipment corrosion, low CO2 loading capacity and weight requirement [9]. Thus, membrane technology has been widely studied as an alternative for CO2 removal processes since it is energy e ffi cient and involved low cost [10,11].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the current study, new types of MMMs are fabricated by incorporating NH2-MIL-53(Al) fi ller into cellulose acetate (CA) polymer. Subsequently, crystallinity, morphology, thermal stability, functional groups and density measurement of the resultant MMMs were veri fi ed by using di ff erent analytical tools. The results showed that the presence of NH2-MIL-53(Al) in CA matrix had enhanced the CO2 permeability, CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 ideal selectivities from 15.5 Barrer to 52.6 Barrer, 8.8 to 23.4 and 10.7 to 28.7, respectively. The present study revealed that the addition of 15 wt% NH2-MIL-53(Al) MOF into the CA matrix resulted in highest enhancement of FFV. Furthermore, MMM loaded with 15 wt% NH2-MIL-53(Al) demonstrated highest CO2 permeability as well as CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 selectivities of 52.6 Barrer, 28.7 and 23.4, respectively. Besides, the resultant MMM showed CO2 plasticization resistance of up to 30 bar, which is 200% increment in comparison of pristine CA membrane which is only 10 bar. This work also revealed that the resultant MMMs showed signi fi cantly higher CO2 separation performance compared to the results previously reported for CA-based MMMs. Thus, it can be concluded that the MMMs fabricated in the present work has potential for CO2 removal from the fl ue gas and natural gas at industrial scale.

Also, these membranes usually su ff er from performance restriction, swelling, fouling and structure instability at high temperature [15]. Along with polymeric membranes, inorganic membranes such as silica [16 -20], carbon [21,22] and zeolite [23 -29] membranes are widely reported for CO2 separation from N2 and CH4 because of their tremendous properties including higher CO2 separation performance and thermal stability [30,31]. However, these membranes are facing challenges such as high cost and complicated fabrication process [32]. Therefore, mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) have been introduced by the addition of inorganic molecular sieve materials into polymers [33]. MMMs demonstrate bene fi ts of inorganic and polymer materials such as high thermal stability and lower cost. Additionally, high separation performance of MMM is another advantage which helps it to cross the tradeo ff limitation.

texts.orig: It is well documented in the literature that the glassy polymers are potential candidate as compared to rubbery polymers for the fabrication of MMMs because of its higher perm-selectivity [41]. Furthermore, among the glassy polymers, an extensive use of CA as the membrane is mainly because of its low manufacturing cost, easy to fabricate and high CO2 solubility [42,43].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Saneepur and his co-workers [43] studied the performance of NaY/ CA MMM in CO2 separation from N2. CO2 permeability of 4.9 Barrer has been achieved, which was 145% higher compared to the CO2 permeability obtained using pristine CA membrane. In another work, they tested functionalized (Co) NaY/CA MMM in CO2/N2 separation. CO2 permeability and CO2/N2 selectivity of 3.5 Barrer and 13 were achieved, respectively [43]. Along with NaY, incorporation of multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) into CA had also been investigated for the fabrication of MMMs in gases separation. Moghadassi and his coworkers [42] reported CO2 permeability of 7.7 Barrer and CO2/CH4 selectivity of 12.72 using MWCNTs/CA MMM. From the literature, it is found that limited researches have been reported on the fabrication of CA-based MMMs; as well as its application in CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 separation.

texts.orig: As of late, metal organic frameworks (MOFs) have been explored as potential fi llers for the fabrication of MMMs [44]. MOFs exhibits higher adsorption capacities, pore volumes and surface area compared to the other fi llers [45]. Besides, organic linker presence in MOFs also enhances its compatibility with polymers and allows higher loading during the fabrication of the membranes [46]. It is well documented in the literature that the amine functionalized MOFs based MMMs showed higher CO2 separation performance compared to pristine MOFs based MMMs [47].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: NH2-MIL-53(Al) is a type of amine functionalized MOF with MIL-53 topology. Fig. 1 shows the 3D structure of NH2-MIL-53(Al) with large pore (lp) and narrow pore (np) shape [48,49]. Referring to Fig. 1, NH2MIL-53(Al) consists of MO4(OH)2 octahedral (M=Al 3+ ), which is connected by free-standing amine group [50]. Furthermore, it possesses diamond shape and aperture of 7.5 Å. Also, NH2-MIL-53(Al) exhibits attractive characteristics for examples; high thermal stability, high pore volumes and large surface areas [47]. On the other hand, terephthalate ligands present in NH2-MIL-53(Al) could enhance its compatibility with polymer matrix [51]. Besides, its fl exibility can also improve the performance of MMMs [52].

texts.orig: Although, several studies have reported on the fabrication of MMMs using NH2-MIL-53(Al); the incorporation of NH2-MIL-53(Al) fi ller into the cellulose acetate (CA) is scarcely found in the literature and its performance in CO2 separation is worth to be investigated. Thus, in the present study, new types of NH2-MIL-53(Al)/CA MMMs are fabricated and the e ff ect of NH2-MIL-53(Al) loading on the performance of NH2MIL-53(Al)/CA MMMs is reported. Subsequently, characterization such as structure, morphology, composition, thermal stability, functional groups and density measurement of the resultant MMMs are studied. In addition, resultant MMMs are preceded for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 separation study.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Fabrication of pristine cellulose acetate (CA) membrane
texts.text: 2.3. Fabrication of pristine cellulose acetate (CA) membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the present work, CA dense membrane was fabricated by the method revealed by Jusoh et al. [62]. Firstly, the CA powder was heated under vacuum at 70 °C overnight for moisture removal. Then, CA was dissolved into NMP solvent to formulate 10% w/v polymer solution. After continuous stirring for 18 h, CA solution was allowed to degas at 25 °C for 12 h. After degassed, the CA solution was fi ltered using 5.0 µm PTFE membrane fi lter, and cast on a petri-dish. Subsequently, membrane heating treatment was conducted at 90 °C for 24 h. In the next step, the resultant membrane was further preceded for annealing at the rate of 10 °C/12 h from 50 °C to 120 °C. Then the resultant membrane was coded as NH2-MIL-(0)-CA. Lastly, resultant membrane was preceded for characterization and gas permeation testing.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.4. Fabrication of NH2-MIL-53(Al)/CA MMMs
texts.text: 2.4. Fabrication of NH2-MIL-53(Al)/CA MMMs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MMMs containing various loadings of NH2-MIL-53(Al) were fabricated by using solution blending method. First, CA was dissolved in NMP solvent under continuous stirring for 18 h. Then, CA/NMP solution was allowed to degas at 25 °C for overnight. Subsequently, 5 wt% of NH2-MIL-53(Al) particles were added into NMP solvent, stirred and sonicated alternately. Next, priming of particles was conducted by adding 10 wt% of CA/NMP solution into the NH2-MIL-53(Al)/NMP solution. Subsequently, stirring of the mixture was conducted for 0.5 h to ensure homogenous dispersion of the particles. Then, the solution mixture was further sonicated at the same time duration. After that, the remaining bulk CA/NMP solution was poured into the primed NH2-MIL53(Al)/CA solution, and it was again stirred and sonicated for 0.5 h, alternatively. This followed by vigorous stirring of the solution mixture for 1 h. Then, the solution mixture was casted onto a level petri dish. After that, the membrane was subjected to heating treatment as described in Section 2.3. The resultant membrane was coded as NH2-MIL(5)-CA. Similar procedure was repeated for the fabrication of MMMs consisted of 10 wt%, 15 wt% and 20 wt% loadings of NH2-MIL-53(Al). Then, the resultant MMMs were preceded for characterization and gas permeation testing.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The crystallinity of the resultant samples were investigated by using X-ray di ff ractometer (Model: X ' Pert3 Powder, PANayltical). The X-ray di ff ractometer was operated at room temperature at 2 theta range from 5° ≥ 2 θ ≥ 45°. The X-ray scans were taken with step size of 0.01° under the vacuum condition. Samples were tested by placing them in front of X-ray tube, where rays emit and hit the materials directly. After this, Xray beams di ff racted in a well pattern and produced peaks. The XRD of the samples were measured by examining at the enunciation of the 2 theta versus intensity by using on set method.

texts.orig: 2.5.3. Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM)
texts.text: 2.5.3. Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Morphology of the resultant membranes was examined by using FESEM (Model: Zeiss Supra 55 VP). Firstly, small pieces of the samples were put into the liquid nitrogen in order to cut the samples with uniform morphology. Subsequently, all samples were cracked and mounted on stub using double-sided carbon tape. Then, gold coating was conducted on each membrane sample in order to produce the clear FESEM images. In the next step, all samples were placed into the aluminium pan and it was performed at 5 kV at room temperature. Meanwhile, composition of particles and MMMs was determined by using energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) attached to FESEM. EDX analysis was conducted on the images of samples having width of 10 μ m.

texts.orig: 2.5.4. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC)
texts.text: 2.5.4. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the current work, di ff erential scanning calorimeter (DSC, Q2000) was utilized to measure the glass transition temperature (Tg) of the resultant samples. Firstly, 3 mg of the each sample were placed into the aluminium pan of DSC. Then the samples were preceded to heat up at 240 °C at a heating rate of 20 °C/min to remove moisture and thermal history. Subsequently, all samples were quenched to 30 °C and heated again to 240 °C under a similar heating condition. Tg of the samples was measured by examining at the enunciation of the heat fl ow versus temperature bend by using on set method.

texts.orig: 2.5.5. Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fourier transform infrared (Model; Perkin-Elmer Spectrum one) was used to determine the existence of functional groups in NH2-MIL-5(Al) particles using KBr pellet method. Approximately 5 mg of NH2-MIL53(Al) particles were blended with KBr and compressed into the pellet. The analysis of the powder sample was conducted in a transmittance mode ranging from 400 cm -1 to 4000 cm -1 . Meanwhile, spectra of membrane samples were acquired by utilizing ATR -FTIR. The spectrum of each membrane was obtained ranging from 650 cm -1 to 4000 cm -1 . Each spectrum of the resultant membranes was accumulating 50 scans with an accuracy of ± 1 °C/cm -1 under the transmission mode.

texts.orig: XRD pattern of NH2-MIL-53(Al) powder and resultant membranes is shown in Fig. 2. From Fig. 2, it can be observed that the powder sample shows XRD peaks at 2 θ values of 9°, 10°, 12°, 15.5°, 18°, 21°, 25° and 27°, which con fi rmed the structure of NH2-MIL-53(Al) [61]. The characteristic peak observed at 9° could be because of the amine group present in the sample. Also, the peaks at 12° and 18° are attributed to the large pore and narrow pore con fi guration of NH2-MIL-53(Al), respectively, as shown in Fig. 1. However, peaks at 9.8° and 15.1° are corresponding to the re fl ection of peaks at 12° and 18°, respectively.

texts.orig: 3.1.4. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping analysis
texts.text: 3.1.4. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 5 shows the EDX mapping results of the resultant MMMs. Referring to Fig. 5, consecutive blue points represent the concentration of Al in the membranes. The structure of NH2-MIL-53(Al) is made up of AlO4(OH)2 octahedral [48,49] and therefore, Al is presented as a major element in the MMMs. From Fig. 5, when the loading of NH2-MIL53(Al) particles in polymer phase is increased, the concentration of Al in MMMs is also increased. On the hand, referring to Fig. 5(d), it has been found that NH2-MIL-53(Al) particles are closely packed together in MMM. These results con fi rmed that the particles were agglomerated in NH2-MIL-(20)-CA MMM. Thus, it can be concluded that EDX mapping results support the FESEM results.

texts.orig: Fig. S2 shows the DSC curves of the resultant membranes. The glass transition temperature (Tg) values of MMMs were reproducible with an error of ± 1 °C. Pristine CA membrane demonstrated Tg of 197.3 °C, which is comparable with those results reported by Seng et al. [71]. The Tg values were enhanced from 197.3 °C to 214.5 °C on the increment of particles loading from 5 wt% to 20 wt% in MMMs. The increment in Tg indicated that the NH2-MIL-53(Al) had increased the sti ff ness of the membranes [72]. Furthermore, the presence of NH2-MIL-53(Al) in MMMs changed the CA structure and restricted the fl exibility of the polymer chains by the interaction with the functional ( e NH2) groups [62,72]. Besides, higher Tg of MMMs re fl ects an e ff ective polymer- fi ller interaction associated with the quality of the interface [47]. From the above discussion, it has also been determined that the increment of fi ller loading into CA polymer increases the thermal stability of MMMs.

texts.text: 3.1.6. Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR)

texts.orig: Fig. 6 shows the FTIR spectrum of NH2-MIL-53(Al) powder and ATR-FTIR spectra of the resultant membranes. Referring to Fig. 6, characteristic bands at 460 cm -1 and 490 cm -1 are due to the aromatic and aliphatic C e H bond of NH2-MIL-53(Al), respectively [48]. The peak at 660 cm -1 is corresponding to the C e OH bond [48]. On the other hand, the bands at 814 cm -1 and 980 cm -1 are due to C e O e C and O e Al bending, respectively [49]. The characteristic peaks at 1440 cm -1 , 1550 cm -1 and 1600 cm -1 are corresponding to the CH2 deformation and symmetric as well as asymmetric stretching mode of carboxylate groups, respectively. The two bands at 3380 cm -1 and 3500 cm -1 are attributed to the presence of e NH2 group and OH bonding, respectively [47].

texts.orig: Meanwhile, ATR-FTIR spectra of CA membranes exhibited the typical vibrational bands in the region of 650 cm -1 to 3650 cm -1 . Referring to Fig. 6, band at 900 cm -1 is detected for the β -linked present in pristine CA structure [43]. Meanwhile, the bands at 1032 cm -1 and 1121 cm -1 are corresponding to the symmetric as well as asymmetric vibration of the C e O bond, respectively [71]. The peaks at 1215 cm -1 , 1367 cm -1 , 1440 cm -1 and 1737 cm -1 are attributed to the acetate C e C e O stretching, CH3 deformation, H e C e H vibration and C ] O stretching, respectively [73]. It can be observed from Fig. 6 that the spectra of NH2-MIL-53(Al)/CA MMMs exhibited the peaks of both NH2-MIL-53(Al) and pristine CA membrane. This result signi fi es the presence of NH2-MIL-53(Al) fi ller in polymer phase. Furthermore, hydrogen bonding (O e H) between e NH2 group and CA polymer is also identi fi ed by characteristic peak at 3500 cm -1 which con fi rmed the strong fi ller-polymer in MMMs. Therefore, it has been concluded that FTIR spectra also supports the FESEM results.

texts.orig: 3.1.7. Free fractional volume (FFV) analysis
texts.text: 3.1.7. Free fractional volume (FFV) analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Density and fractional free volume (FFV) of the resultant membranes are listed in Table 3. Referring to Table 3, pristine CA membrane demonstrated the highest density of 1.253 g/cm 3 and lowest FFV of 0.1614 compared to the MMMs fabricated in the current study. This result shows that the incorporation of the particles into CA polymer has enhanced the free volume cavities and changed the regular packing of polymer matrix and thus, resulted in higher FFV [74,75]. Referring to Table 3, increment of NH2-MIL-53 (Al) loading from 5 wt% to 15 wt% in the CA matrix has resulted in the increment of FFV of the membrane and MMM loaded with 15 wt% of particles showed the highest of FFV of 0.2768. This result reveals that the presence of appropriate amount of NH2-MIL-53(Al) in CA matrix could change the polymer packing which can subsequently developed the accessible cavities and thus, increase the value of FFV. However, further increase in loading of NH2-MIL53(Al) from 15 wt% to 20 wt% has slightly reduced the FFV of the membrane from 0.2768 to 0.2698. This result could be because of cluster formation of NH2-MIL-53(Al) crystals as shown in Fig. 4(e), which might block or narrowed the free volume cavities of CA matrix [76].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Single gas permeation results of NH2-MIL-53(Al)/CA MMMs are shown in Fig. 7. The data was reproduced with ± 3% at feed pressure and temperature of 3 bar and 25 °C, respectively. Referring to Fig. 7(a), CO2, N2 and CH4 permeabilities of 15.5 Barrer, 1.7 Barrer and 1.4 Barrer are obtained for pristine CA membrane, respectively. It can also be observed from Fig. 7 that the permeabilities of gases are decreasing as follows; CO2 > N2 > CH4. The decreasing trend is attributed to the di ff erence in solubility of gases in CA polymer [42]. From Fig. 7(b), CO2/CH4 as well as CO2/N2 ideal selectivities of 10.7 and 8.8 are achieved for pristine CA membrane, respectively. It is also revealed that the selectivities are decreased in the sequence of CO2/CH4 > CO2/N2. This decreasing trend could be because of smaller di ff usion of CH4 in the polymer matrix and its lower a ffi nity for Langmuir sorption sites compared to the CO2 and N2 [77]. Hence, CH4 permeability is lower than N2 permeability, and thus, CO2/CH4 ideal selectivity is higher than the CO2/N2 ideal selectivity of the resultant membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Furthermore, gases permeability as well as CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 ideal selectivities obtained for the pristine CA membrane fabricated using NMP solvent is higher than those results reported for pristine CA membrane fabricated using THF and acetone solvents [42,71,78]. This result is mainly attributed to the e ff ect of NMP solvent which had higher molar volume and thus, enhanced the FFV of the polymer matrix compared to the other solvents [79]. Besides, NMP also a ff ects the acetate C e C e O stretching which contributes to the restriction of neighbouring C e OH rings [79]. Thus, the interaction between CA polymer and the gases increased. Furthermore, higher value of FFV reported in Section 3.1.7 also supports the permeation results obtained for pristine CA membrane. Hence, in the current work, pristine CA membrane fabricated using NMP solvent showed higher separation performance compared to the pristine CA membrane fabricated using THF and acetone reported in the literature [42,71,78].

texts.orig: As shown in Fig. 7(a), CO2 and N2 permeabilities ranging from 24.7 Barrer to 52.6 Barrer and 2.1 Barrer to 2.2 Barrer, respectively, are attained for NH2-MIL-53(Al)/CA MMMs. Meanwhile, MMMs demonstrated CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 ideal selectivities ranging from 12.2 to 23.4 and 16.5 to 28.7, respectively. Referring to Fig. 7 also, it can be found that MMMs show higher permeabilities and selectivities compared to pristine CA membrane. The increment in the performance of NH2-MIL-53(Al)/CA MMMs could be due to the addition of NH2-MIL53(Al) into CA which increases the CO2 transport through the membrane and thus, separation performance [49]. Fig. 8 shows the possible transport mechanism of gases through NH2-MIL-53(Al)/CA MMM. Referring to Fig. 8, CO2 transport is facilitated by the reversible interaction between CO2 and the amine groups [80]. The large pore volume of NH2-MIL-53(Al) fi ller also contributed to the increment in CO2 transportation through MMMs [55]. Furthermore, the addition of NH2-MIL-53(Al) into CA has increased the FFV of MMMs which also enhanced the solution-di ff usion mechanism of CO2 through the MMM [49]. Besides, the functional ( e NH2) groups presented in the fi ller can enhance the compatibility between CA polymer chains and fi ller attributed to hydrogen bonding [55]. Therefore, non-selective voids in the MMMs could be reduced and thus, increased the gas pair selectivity.

texts.text: 3.2.2. Mixed gas separation performance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since NH2-MIL-(15)-CA MMM showed the highest CO2/N2 and CO2/ CH4 ideal selectivities, it was further preceded for mixed gas permeation test. In the current study, CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 of 50 vol%/50 vol %, respectively, were used as feed gases. Table 4 shows the pure gas permeation and mixed gas separation results of 15 wt% NH2-MIL53(Al)/CA MMM. Referring to Table 4, it can be observed that CO2, N2 and CH4 permeabilities as well as CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 selectivities of MMM in mixed gas testing were lower than that of pure gas. The lower performance of the MMM in mixed gas separation is possibly due to the competition e ff ects of one gas component over the other gas component for the sorption and transport penetration over the membrane [82]. As a result, CO2 permeation reduced and thus, CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 selectivities of the membrane were also decreased.
texts.text: Since NH2-MIL-(15)-CA MMM showed the highest CO2/N2 and CO2/ CH4 ideal selectivities, it was further preceded for mixed gas permeation test. In the current study, CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 of 50 vol%/50 vol %, respectively, were used as feed gases. Table 4 shows the pure gas permeation and mixed gas separation results of 15 wt% NH2-MIL53(Al)/CA MMM. Referring to Table 4, it can be observed that CO2, N2 and CH4 permeabilities as well as CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 selectivities of MMM in mixed gas testing were lower than that of pure gas. The lower performance of the MMM in mixed gas separation is possibly due to the competition e ff ects of one gas component over the other gas component for the sorption and transport penetration over the membrane [82]. As a result, CO2 permeation reduced and thus, CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 selectivities of the membrane were also decreased.

texts.orig: On the other hand, NH2-MIL-(15)-CA MMM demonstrated CO2 plasticization resistance at 30 bar, which is 200% higher compared to those value obtained for pristine CA membrane. This result is could be due to the presence of the fi ller, which enhanced the CO2 permeability of the membrane and thus, improved CO2 plasticization resistance [62]. Decreasing CO2 permeability trend of MMM up to 30 bar feed pressure suggests that the adsorbed CO2 has a strong interaction with the amine group of NH2-MIL-53(Al) [54]. On the increment of feed pressure, CO2 adopts facilitated transport mechanism arising from the interaction between CO2 and the amine group of fi ller [80]. However, CO2 permeability was reduced on the further increment of feed pressure from 30 bar to 38 bar, could be because of the ' amine carrier saturation ' phenomenon, a typical facilitated transport characteristic [83]. With increasing feed pressure, more and more amine carriers were involved in the interactions with CO2 until all the carriers were interacted

texts.orig: 3.3. Comparison with Robeson upper bound relationship
texts.text: 3.3. Comparison with Robeson upper bound relationship

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figs. 10 and 11 show the comparison of membrane separation performance obtained in the present work with those results reported for CA-based MMMs via CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 Robeson ' s upper bound limits. Fig. 10 revealed that the CO2/CH4 separation performance of NH2-MIL-53(Al)/CA MMMs lie below the Robeson upper bound limit [13,14]. Meanwhile, CA-based MMMs in CO2/CH4 separation reported in the literature were also failed to surpass and far below the upper bound limit [42,86]. Furthermore, NH2-MIL-53(Al)/CA MMMs fabricated in the current study and CA-based MMMs reported in the literature [42,43,71,87] were also unable to exceed the CO2/N2 Robeson ' s upper bound limit [13].
texts.text: Figs. 10 and 11 show the comparison of membrane separation performance obtained in the present work with those results reported for CA-based MMMs via CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 Robeson ' s upper bound limits. Fig. 10 revealed that the CO2/CH4 separation performance of NH2-MIL-53(Al)/CA MMMs lie below the Robeson upper bound limit [13,14]. Meanwhile, CA-based MMMs in CO2/CH4 separation reported in the literature were also failed to surpass and far below the upper bound limit [42,86]. Furthermore, NH2-MIL-53(Al)/CA MMMs fabricated in the current study and CA-based MMMs reported in the literature [42,43,71,87] were also unable to exceed the CO2/N2 Robeson ' s upper bound limit [13].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Apart from that, Figs. 10 and 11 also show the comparison between the separation performance obtained in current work with the other related membranes reported in the literature [42,43,71,87]. NH2-MIL53(Al)/CA MMMs fabricated in the present work showed higher CO2 permeability as well as CO2/CH4 ideal selectivity compared to CO2/CH4 separation performance of MWCNTs-CA [42] and SAHM-3-CA [86] MMMs reported in the literature. Subsequently, resultant MMMs also showed higher CO2 permeability and CO2/N2 ideal selectivity compared to those results reported in the literature for MWCNTs/CA [42], NaY/CA [43] and Co-NaY/CA [43] MMMs. From the comparison, it has been found that the incorporation of NH2-MIL-53(Al) fi ller into CA has successfully enhanced the gases separation performance of CA membrane.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In summary, CA-based MMMs were fabricated using various loadings of NH2-MIL-53(Al). The crystallinity, morphology, functional groups and fractional free volume of the resultant powder and membranes have been con fi rmed by using analytical tools. DSC investigation found that the Tg of MMMs was enhanced by increasing the NH2-MIL53(Al) MOF loading in CA polymer. Subsequently, increasing in MOF loading has resulted in the increment of FFV of MMMs. furthermore, MMM loaded with 15 wt% of NH2-MIL-53(Al) demonstrated highest CO2 permeability, CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 ideal selectivities of 52.6, 23.4 and 28.7, respectively. The gas permeation results obtained were correlated well with the characterization results. Besides, NH2-MIL-(15)CA membrane exhibited plasticization resistance up to 30 bar, which was 200% higher than that the value obtained for pristine CA membrane. Subsequently, it also found that the separation performance of NH2-MIL-(15)-CA membrane is signi fi cantly higher compared to the results previously reported for CA-based MMMs. Thus, it can be concluded that the MMMs fabricated in the present work has potential for CO2 removal from the fl ue gas and natural gas at industrial scale.
texts.text: In summary, CA-based MMMs were fabricated using various loadings of NH2-MIL-53(Al). The crystallinity, morphology, functional groups and fractional free volume of the resultant powder and membranes have been con fi rmed by using analytical tools. DSC investigation found that the Tg of MMMs was enhanced by increasing the NH2-MIL53(Al) MOF loading in CA polymer. Subsequently, increasing in MOF loading has resulted in the increment of FFV of MMMs. furthermore, MMM loaded with 15 wt% of NH2-MIL-53(Al) demonstrated highest CO2 permeability, CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 ideal selectivities of 52.6, 23.4 and 28.7, respectively. The gas permeation results obtained were correlated well with the characterization results. Besides, NH2-MIL-(15)CA membrane exhibited plasticization resistance up to 30 bar, which was 200% higher than that the value obtained for pristine CA membrane. Subsequently, it also found that the separation performance of NH2-MIL-(15)-CA membrane is signi fi cantly higher compared to the results previously reported for CA-based MMMs. Thus, it can be concluded that the MMMs fabricated in the present work has potential for CO2 removal from the fl ue gas and natural gas at industrial scale.
